text:
Cryogenic separation of noble gases such as Xenon (Xe) and Krypton (Kr) is an energy and cost-intensive process, particularly when dealing with dilute feedstocks. While certain metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) functionalized with polarizing groups are theoretically known to be excellent solid-state adsorbents for Xe/Kr separation, there is limited information as to the fundamental nature of the xenon–framework interaction. A new microporous calcium-based MOF, SBMOF-2, is reported here that exhibited high selectivity for Xe toward Kr at ambient conditions. The Xe/Kr selectivity value for SBMOF-2 was approximately 10 at 298 K and the xenon uptake of the material was 27.07 wt %.

The selectivity of this sorbent material towards Xe over Kr is assigned using single crystal X-ray diffraction to the specificity of the cage environments in the SBMOF-2 structure made of phenyl rings and occupied preferentially by Xe due to the higher abundance of polar -OH species at these sites. Their performance in separating Xe from Kr was confirmed experimentally by breakthrough studies as well as theoretically by calculations indicating that their performance is comparable to that of other of the best unfunctionalized MOFs such as NiMOF-74 and cobalt formate frameworks but with a greater thermal stability and less weight.
